original_hypothesis.title: _afterTokenTransfer Missing Break After Swap-and-Pop Skips Swapped Element
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In _afterTokenTransfer, when removing a holder via swap-and-pop (copying last element to position i, then pop), the loop does not break after removal. After swap-and-pop, `i` increments, causing the element that was swapped from the end into position i to be skipped entirely — it is never checked. This means: (1) if there are duplicate entries of the same address (from bug TECH-H001/H002), only the first is removed per call; (2) the swapped element may be incorrectly left in the array even if it should be checked for removal conditions.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The missing break is a demonstrable code defect. Combined with the duplicate entry bugs, this creates a situation where phantom entries can never be fully cleaned up, compounding the DoS risk.

proof_of_signal.pattern: After swap-and-pop: holders[i] = holders[holders.length - 1]; holders.pop(); — no break statement; i++ on next iteration skips the swapped element

preconditions: An address with 0 post-transfer balance exists in holders
The address is not the last element in the array

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Swap-and-pop without break; loop continues and skips swapped element
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Element swapped from end to position i is at index i but loop advances to i+1

analysis_questions: Under what conditions does the skipped element matter?
Does this compound with the address(0) bug to prevent cleanup?
Can the swapped element be a different address that should remain, causing accidental removal?
validation_requirements: Create scenario where address appears multiple times, transfer all tokens away, verify cleanup removes only one entry
Verify that the last element swapped in is never checked in subsequent iterations

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Combined with TECH-H002: multiple address(0) entries exist. A mint to address(0)-adjacent entries triggers _afterTokenTransfer for from=address(0). Only the first address(0) occurrence is removed. The swapped element (which might also be address(0)) is skipped. Phantom entries persist and accumulate, compounding DoS risk.
prerequisites: Duplicate entries in holders array (from TECH-H001 or TECH-H002)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Persistent phantom entries cause gas waste and potential permanent distribution DoS
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders affected by degraded distribution
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No recovery mechanism for corrupted holders array

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for swap-and-pop behavior with duplicate entries

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Gas overhead per phantom entry in distribution

validation_result.impact_summary: Fails to properly remove duplicate entries from the holders array, leaving phantom entries that permanently waste gas and compound the risk of a distribution DoS.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: In `_afterTokenTransfer`, when a user's balance reaches 0, the contract attempts to remove them from the `holders` array using the swap-and-pop pattern. However, the loop lacks a `break;` statement or an `i--;` decrement after the swap. As a result, the loop variable `i` increments on the next iteration, completely skipping the element that was just swapped from the end of the array into position `i`. If the `holders` array contains duplicate entries for the same address (which is possible due to the `address(0)` burn bug or zero-value transfers), and one of those duplicates is swapped into position `i`, it will be skipped and left in the array permanently. Even without duplicates, the missing `break` causes the loop to unnecessarily iterate through the rest of the array, wasting gas.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code clearly shows a `for` loop iterating over `holders.length`. Inside the loop, if `holders[i] == from`, it performs `holders[i] = holders[holders.length - 1]; holders.pop();`. There is no `break` to exit the loop, nor is there an `i--` to re-evaluate the newly swapped element at index `i`. This is a textbook anti-pattern for array removal.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The swap-and-pop operation replaces the element at index `i` with the last element of the array. Because the loop continues and `i` increments, the new element at index `i` is never checked. If the swapped element is also `from` (due to duplicates), it escapes removal.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When a token transfer causes `from`'s balance to reach 0, `_afterTokenTransfer` iterates through `holders`. It finds `from` at index `i`, replaces it with the last element, and pops the array. The loop then increments `i` to `i + 1`, skipping the element that was just moved to index `i`. If the array was `[A, B, C, A]` and `from` is `A`, at `i=0`, `A` is replaced by the last element `A`. The array becomes `[A, B, C]`. The loop increments `i` to 1, skipping the `A` now at index 0. The duplicate `A` remains in the array permanently.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An attacker exploits the `address(0)` burn bug to inflate the `holders` array with multiple `address(0)` entries. Later, a mint operation triggers `_afterTokenTransfer` with `from = address(0)`. The loop attempts to remove `address(0)`. Because of the missing break/decrement, if an `address(0)` is swapped from the end of the array into the current index, it is skipped. This guarantees that duplicate phantom entries cannot be fully cleaned up, accelerating the array bloat and leading to an eventual Out-Of-Gas Denial of Service during `distributeToAllHolders()`.
validation_result.impact_assessment: High. This defect directly compounds other vulnerabilities (like duplicate holder entries) by preventing their cleanup. It guarantees that phantom entries will persist, permanently increasing the gas cost of distributions and eventually bricking the protocol's core reward mechanism via block gas limit exhaustion.
recommended_mitigations: If an address is only expected to appear once, add a `break;` statement immediately after `holders.pop();`.
If an address might appear multiple times and all instances should be removed, add `i--;` after `holders.pop();` to ensure the swapped element is evaluated on the next iteration.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Mint tokens to User A. 2. Exploit a duplicate entry bug (e.g., zero-value transfers) to add User A to the `holders` array multiple times, ensuring one instance is at the end of the array. 3. Transfer all of User A's tokens to User B, triggering `_afterTokenTransfer`. 4. Assert that User A is still present in the `holders` array despite having a balance of 0.
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz testing on the `holders` array manipulation to ensure all edge cases of swap-and-pop (e.g., removing the last element, removing duplicates) behave correctly.
